_meta.description: Content schema for Fantasy Run For A Million editorial articles. Currently placeholder structure. Future: replace with Sanity CMS, Contentful, or markdown files in /content/articles/.

article_schema.description: Schema for a single article — every field here maps to a CMS field or markdown front matter key

fields.title: string — full display title
fields.seo_title: string — optimized for search (often same as title, max 60 chars)
fields.discipline: string | null — reining, cow-horse, cutting, or null for cross-discipline
fields.excerpt: string — 1-2 sentence description for cards and meta description
fields.body: string — markdown content (future: stored in CMS or .md file)
fields.author: string — byline
rider_references: array of rider id strings — enables internal linking
tags: array of keyword strings for SEO and filtering

placeholder_articles.title: Understanding Reining Maneuver Scores
placeholder_articles.seo_title: Reining Maneuver Scoring Explained | Fantasy Run For A Million
placeholder_articles.excerpt: Reining judges score every maneuver individually on a scale from -1.5 to +1.5. Understanding which elements carry the most weight helps fantasy fans identify riders with the highest scoring potential.
placeholder_articles.author: Fantasy Run For A Million Editorial
placeholder_articles.seo_description: Learn how reining maneuver scores work and how they translate to fantasy points. A guide to NRHA pattern scoring for western horse fantasy fans.

placeholder_articles.title: Cow Horse Composite Scoring Explained
placeholder_articles.seo_title: Cow Horse Composite Scoring | Fantasy Run For A Million
placeholder_articles.excerpt: Cow horse competition is scored across three distinct phases — reined work, fence work, and cow work — with scores compiled into a composite result. Fantasy teams should prioritize all-around ability over single-phase specialists.
placeholder_articles.author: Fantasy Run For A Million Editorial
placeholder_articles.seo_description: How cow horse composite scoring works across reined work, fence work, and cow work — and what three-phase competition means for your fantasy team picks.
